segments.s526-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'appeared/rose' (here used with celestial imagery). سر here means 'summit/top' (a metaphorical 'head' of the sun). از تیغ کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the ridge/point of the mountain' (تیغ used as 'point/ridge'), so the whole unit means 'when the sun's summit rose from the mountain's ridge.' Word order is poetic: conjunction + verb + subject + source phrase.
segments.s526-g74: آمد is the simple past 'came'. به سوی + place (صلخر) marks direction 'toward Istakhr' (به here = 'towards/into'). از پیش آب literally 'from before the water' — 'from in front of/over the water' — so this clause says the (previously mentioned) sun came toward Istakhr coming from before the water. The subject (the sun) is implied from the previous segment.
segments.s526-g75: خبر شد is an idiom: 'news came/it became news' = 'news reached'. بر marks the recipient (to); thus بر بهمن اردوان means 'to Bahman Ardavan'. شد is the past of 'become/happen' used here to indicate reception of information rather than a stative 'become'.
segments.s526-g76: دلش گشت is 'his heart became' (گشت = became/turned). پردرد و تیره‌روان are two predicative adjectives joined by و: 'full of pain/sorrow and dark‑minded/gloomy.' The possessive enclitic ش attaches to دل to mean 'his heart.'
segments.s526-g77: نکرد is the past negative 'did not do'. ایچ is an emphatic old/poetic form meaning 'anything' or 'at all' (here in negative: 'not at all'). بر تخت شاهی درنگ means 'delay on the royal throne' (بر = 'on', تخت شاهی = 'royal throne', درنگ = 'delay/linger'). The clause says he did not linger at the throne at all.
segments.s526-g78: بیاورد is past 'brought' (third person). سپاهی is a collective noun 'soldiers/army' (object), and با ساز جنگ 'with the instruments of war' (با = 'with', ساز = 'instrument/tune' here 'military instruments or martial music', جنگ = 'war'). Together: 'he brought an army with the instruments/sounds of war.' Word order places the object before the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s526-g41: هرانکس که بود اندر آن انجمن is a relative/indefinite clause: هرانکس 'whoever' (sentence-initial indefinite pronoun) is linked by the relative particle که to the past-tense copulaبود 'was'. اندر آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase (اندر = 'in/within') with آن انجمن 'that assembly' as its object; literal order is 'whoever that was in that assembly'.
segments.s526-g42: ز شمشیر زن مرد و از رای‌زن lists the kinds of people present using the poetic/prepositional ز/از 'from/of'. شمشیر زن is a compound where زن is agentive 'one who strikes/uses' (i.e. 'swordsman'), مرد names the person; و introduces the second class with از رای‌زن 'from/of the counsellors'. The construction is elliptical (a list of types) and uses پرِ poetic ز/از instead of modern از alone.
segments.s526-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آواز بشنید is an object (آواز 'voice/call') plus past verb بشنید 'heard' (classical past, 3rd person); subject is contextually plural but omitted. بر پای خاست is an idiomatic past phrase 'rose to (their) feet' where بر = 'on/upon', پای = 'foot', and خاست is the archaic past form 'rose'.
segments.s526-g44: همه is the explicit subject 'everyone/all'. راز دل is a genitive phrase 'secret of (the) heart' (راز governed by دل). بازگفتند is past-plural 'they spoke/related/revealed' (باز- here intensifies or indicates 'out/spoken'), and راست at the end functions as an adverb 'truly/straightforwardly', modifying بازگفتند; word order places the adverb after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s526-g45: که is the complementizer introducing reported content from the previous clause. هرکس که هستیم literally 'whichever/whoever we are' uses هرکس + relative که and the present plural copula هستیم 'we are'; the phrase is appositive/identificatory and is completed by the noun بابک‌نژاد which names the group ('descendants of Babak'). The clause is somewhat elliptical: بابک‌نژاد functions as the predicate/national-lineage identifier of 'we'.
segments.s526-g46: به دیدار و چهر تو is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight/meeting and face of you' (به = 'at/for', دیدار = 'sight/meeting', چهر = 'face', تو = 'you'). گشتیم is the past tense of گشتن used copulatively 'we became/turned', and شاد 'happy' is the predicate adjective. Word order is PSV: prepositional phrase → verb → predicate adjective.
segments.s526-g47: و دیگر که هستیم ساسانیان: و دیگر 'and the rest/others' or 'and besides', followed by the complementizer که and the present copula هستیم 'we are', with ساسانیان ('the Sasanian [people]') as the predicate nominal. The clause asserts group identity for the remaining speakers; دیگر functions adverbially ('the others/aside from that').
segments.s526-g48: ببندیم is a hortative/first-person-plural present/subjunctive 'let us bind/gird' (classical form with prefix ب‑). کین را is the definite object 'vengeance' marked by را. کمر بر میان is the locative phrase 'a belt upon the middle/waist' (کمر here 'girdle/belt' rather than abstract 'strength'), so the image is 'gird (about) our middles a belt of vengeance.' Word order places object with را before the locative phrase and verb, as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s526-g17: دگر (here) marks 'other/others' (contrastive) and precedes the relative هرک; هرک is an indefinite relative meaning 'whoever'. از + تخم is a genitive-like construction 'from the seed/of the stock (of...)' where تخم means 'origin/lineage'. دارا here names the ancestor (Dara) whose progeny are meant; بدند is the past plural of بودن used predicatively ('they were/became'). Note the poetic omission of the ezāfe between تخم and دارا.
segments.s526-g18: به هر کشوری is a distributive phrase 'in/throughout every country' with به functioning as a directional/prepositional marker. هر is 'each/every', کشوری 'country/realm' (adjectival/attributive form), نامدارا is a poetic form meaning 'renowned/famous', and بدند repeats the past-plural verb 'they were/became' agreeing with the implied subject 'they' (the descendants).
segments.s526-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی here means 'news/information' (the announcement that arrives). آمد is the past tense 'came'. ز (poetic form of از) indicates source 'of/from', followed by شاه اردشیر 'King Ardashir' (proper name); the whole phrase = 'when news came of King Ardashir'.
segments.s526-g20: ز شادی here means 'because of/from joy'. جوان شد is a compound predicate 'became young' (جوان = 'young', شد = 'became'). دل مرد پیر is a possessive/nominal phrase 'the heart of the old man' — in poetry the ezāfe is omitted, so دل (heart) is followed by مرد پیر (the old man) rather than دلِ مردِ پیر.
segments.s526-g21: همی + رفت is a poetic continuous or iterative past: 'kept coming/going'. مردم is the subject 'people'. ز followed by دریا و کوه marks origin 'from sea and mountain' (z = poetic 'from'). The verb رفت here carries motion meaning and with همی gives an ongoing arrival from those places.
segments.s526-g22: به نزدیک is an adverbial phrase 'toward/nearby' indicating approach. برنا is an adjective/noun 'young (men/youth)' modifying the following groups; گروها گروه is a doubled, emphatic nominal sequence 'bands upon bands / group after group' (گروها = plural/collective form, followed by گروه 'group'), a common poetic repetition to stress multitude. The phrase describes numerous youthful bands drawing near.
segments.s526-g23: ز هر شهر = 'from each/every city'. فرزانه‌ای is an indefinite singular 'a wise one/sage' (note the -ای ending). رای‌زن is a compound noun 'counselor/advisor' (one who gives counsel). The whole segment describes agents (wise counselors) coming from each city.
segments.s526-g24: به نزد = 'to/in the presence of'. جهانجوی ('world-seeker') is an epithet for the addressee or central figure meaning 'one who seeks the world/realm' (an ambitious man). گشت here is the past of گشتن used as 'became/was formed' or 'turned into', so گشت انجمن means 'an assembly was formed' (the verbal predicate applies to the gathering of those counselors). Note again the terser, poetic word order without explicit particles.
segments.s526-g25: زبان برگشاد is an idiom: زبان (literally “tongue”) + برگشاد “opened” = “opened his tongue,” i.e., began to speak. اردشیر جوان follows in apposition (name + epithet), with جوان as an adjective after the proper name (typical Persian word order: name then epithet).
segments.s526-g26: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/interjection (“O” / “you who are”), addressing the addressees; نامداران روشن‌روان is a compound address: نامداران “renowned (ones)” + روشن‌روان “bright‑souled / clear‑minded,” both adjectival nouns describing those addressed.
segments.s526-g27: کسی نیست = “there is no one.” زین نامدار انجمن is a genitival/partitive phrase “of these famed (men) of the assembly” (زین here is a poetic/formal marker meaning ‘of this/these’ or related; it precedes the nouns it qualifies). Together the segment sets up the negative existential subject.
segments.s526-g28: ز فرزانه و مردم رای‌زن: ز (poetic ‘from/among’) introduces the traits being denied; فرزانه “wise/sage” and مردم رای‌زن “people’s counselor” are coordinated nouns describing the kind of person claimed not to be found among those famed assembly members.
segments.s526-g29: که introduces a relative/exclamatory clause “who/that”; نشنید is past negative of شنیدن and here means “has not heard / is not aware of” (negated perfective sense); کاسکندر بدگمان is the subject phrase “Alexander, the suspicious (one).” Word order places the verb before the subject (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s526-g30: چه کرد = “what (he) did” (چه interrogative/exclamative + past verb کرد). از introduces the domain/quality “of” or “on account of” (here: “what he did of/from meanness”); فرومایگی is the noun “meanness / baseness,” and در جهان “in the world” is the locative phrase modifying کرد.
segments.s526-g31: نیاکان ما را یکایک بکشت is a past action: نیاکان ما را (our ancestors — نیاکان with the object marker را) is the object, یکایک “one by one” is an adverbial, and بکشت is an archaic past verb meaning “he killed / he slew” (compound of b‑ + کشت). Word order: object before verb (common in Persian poetic prose).
segments.s526-g32: به بیدادی آورد گیتی به مشت: آورد is the past verb “brought/caused”; گیتی is its direct object “the world.” به بیدادی is an adverbial/measure “into injustice/tyranny” (literally ‘to injustice’), and به مشت “into the fist” is a figurative prepositional phrase meaning ‘under his (cruel) control’ — به here = ‘into/towards.’
segments.s526-g1: وزین سو به دریا رسید اردشیر is an adverbial phrase + past verb + postposed subject, a common poetic order: وزین سو (an archaic/poetic locative phrase = 'from this/that side') + به دریا ('into/to the sea') frames the direction, رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached', and اردشیر (the subject) appears after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s526-g2: به یزدان چنین گفت introduces direct speech: به یزدان is a dative/prepositional phrase 'to God', چنین گفت is a formulaic past 'said thus'. The following کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and دستگیر is used as a vocative epithet 'helper/succoring one'.
segments.s526-g3: تو کردی مرا ایمن از بدکنش is built as Subject (تو) + past transitive verb کردی ('you made') + object م(را) (clitic + object marker) followed by the result/complement ایمن ('safe') and the ablative phrase از بدکنش ('from the evildoer'). In other words, 'you made me safe from the evildoer.' The object clitic is split in the edition (م + را).
segments.s526-g4: که هرگز مبیناد نیکی تنش is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/so that'); هرگز sets the negative 'never', and مبیناد is an archaic/poetic present/subjunctive form meaning 'be seen/appear' (used here with the sense 'may appear' or 'is seen'). نیکی تنش is a genitival phrase 'the goodness (or good deed) of his person' (نیکی modified by the possessor تنش). Overall it expresses a result/wish: 'that the goodness of his person may never be seen.'
segments.s526-g5: برآسود و ملاح را پیش خواند contains the past-intransitive verb برآسود ('he was relieved/at rest' or 'he rested') followed by و ('and') linking to a transitive clause ملاح را پیش خواند: ملاح (the helmsman) + را (definite object marker) + پیش خواند ('called/summoned forward' — پیش + خواند together form 'call forward'). The subject is understood from previous context and appears after the verb phrases in conventional epic narration.
segments.s526-g6: ز کار گذشته فراوان براند is an ablative/temporal phrase z + noun + participial/adjectival past (ز کار گذشته = 'with the work past' / 'after much had been done') followed by فراوان (adverbially 'much' / 'plentifully') and براند, a poetic/past form of راندن meaning 'he drove' or 'he propelled (on)'. The construction gives a cause or circumstance ('after much work was done, he drove on').
segments.s526-g7: نگه کرد فرزانه ملاح پیر uses the light verb کرد with نگه ('a look/gaze') to form 'he looked' (نگه کرد). The nominal phrase فرزانه ملاح پیر follows as the subject/content: 'the wise helmsman, the old (man)', with فرزانه and پیر as attributive adjectives to ملاح. Word order (verb then noun phrase) is common in epic style.
segments.s526-g8: به بالا و چهر و بر اردشیر strings three directions/targets with coordinating و: به بالا ('upward/toward above') + و چهر ('and (his) face/visage' — چهر = face/appearance) + و بر اردشیر ('and toward Ardashir'). The prepositions (به, بر) mark direction/target; the phrase as a whole means he raised his eyes and directed his countenance toward Ardashir.
segments.s526-g65: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that/thereupon' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after'). کنی is the classical 2nd-person verb form (present/subjunctive/imperative sense) of کردن used here as a hortatory/imperative 'do, wage'; رزم (object) precedes the verbal phrase (typical Persian verb-object order is flexible in poetry). با اردوان is the prepositional complement 'against/with Ardavan' (با = 'with/against' here = 'against the foe'); the whole segment issues an instruction to begin hostilities.
segments.s526-g66: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause ('for' / 'that'). اختر جوانست is a nominal predicate: اختر (here a person or epithet 'Akhtar'/'star') + جوانست, where -ست is the archaic 3rd-sg. copula meaning 'is young'. The following و خسرو جوان repeats the predicate with the noun then adjective (no copula required in Persian), 'and Khosrow is young.'
segments.s526-g67: که begins an explanatory clause ('for'). او is the subject pronoun 'he'. از here is the comparative/from marker ('than' or 'from among') linking او to ملوک طوایف ('the kings of the tribes') — a typical classical comparative structure. به گنج is a prepositional phrase 'in/with respect to treasure' that will supply the measure of comparison (he is greater with respect to treasure).
segments.s526-g68: فزونست is a 3rd-person singular predicate ('is more/exceeds'), with the archaic -ست copula; it links back to به گنج (i.e. 'is richer in treasure'). و is the coordinating 'and'. ز (poetic form of از) introduces source/agent 'from him'. دیدی here is the past 3rd-person of دیدن used impersonally/predictively ('was seen' / 'there was seen'); thus زو دیدی آزار و رنج = 'from him there was seen harm and suffering.'
segments.s526-g69: چو is a temporal/conditional 'when' or 'if'. برداشتی is the verb 'take away/remove' in the 2nd-person form; in classical narrative this past/2nd form often introduces a conditional temporal clause ('if/when you remove'). گاه here means 'place/seat' (time/occasion can also be 'گاه', but context gives 'place/post'); او را includes the object marker -را marking گاه as definite/accused ('his place'). ز جای 'from the place' completes the idea 'remove his seat from its place.'
segments.s526-g70: ندارد is the negative 3rd-person singular of داشتن 'does not have/there is not'. کسی 'anyone' is the subject of the negative. زین (poetic ز + این) literally 'from this' or 'of this' and here uses the listed literal sense 'saddle/trappings' (a metonym for one's contingent support). سپس 'after that/thereupon' sets the temporal scope. با تو پای literally 'a foot with you' uses پای metaphorically for 'standing/support' — so the clause says 'no one after that will have a footing/support with you.'
segments.s526-g71: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. بشنید is the past 3rd-person of شنیدن ('he heard'). The epithet گردن‌فراز ('neck-raiser') is an attributive adjective meaning 'proud/haughty' placed before the proper name اردشیر; Persian poetry commonly places epithets in front of names. The clause puts Ardashir as the one who hears the following remarks.
segments.s526-g72: سخنهای is the plural construct 'words/speeches' (the plural suffix attached). بایسته is an adjectival qualifier meaning 'fitting/proper' (here 'appropriate' rather than merely 'necessary'), و 'and', دلپذیر 'pleasant' — the two adjectives jointly qualify the noun: 'proper and pleasing words.' In Persian the adjective follows the noun or can follow it as a compound attribute in poetry.
segments.s526-g57: بران is an archaic/poetic locative particle meaning 'upon/thereon' placed before the object; مهتران is the plural 'chiefs/elders' (here used as the object of praise); آفرین functions as a noun 'praise/acclamation' rather than the verb 'to create'; گسترید is a past-tense verb (classical third-person) 'spread/uttered'. The clause literally reads 'upon them (بران) the chiefs (مهتران) praise uttered/spread'.
segments.s526-g58: This is a causal/locative clause with the verb at the end, typical of classical Persian. به دل در = 'in/into the heart' (the poet doubles locative particles for emphasis), ز introduces the source 'from', اندیشه کین = 'the thought of hatred' (ز + noun = 'from/because of'), and گسترید is the past tense 'spread'. The whole means 'hatred spread in the heart from that thought'.
segments.s526-g59: به نزدیک دریا is a prepositional phrase 'near the sea' with به + نزدیک + noun (a common poetic ordering). یکی before شارستان is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing the town). Word order places the adverbial/locative phrase first for setting the scene.
segments.s526-g60: پی‌افگند is a classical compound verb meaning 'laid down/founded' (here 'founded/erected'); و is the coordinating 'and/then'; شد is the past 'became'; شارستان is 'town/city' repeated as the subject; کارستان is a noun 'marvel/feat' describing the result. The verb-second clause 'و شد شارستان کارستان' = 'and the town became a marvel'.
segments.s526-g61: یکی موبدی = 'a certain mobad (Zoroastrian priest)' (یکی as indefinite introducer). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; با here means 'to' or 'with' marking the addressee, so گفت با اردشیر = 'said to Ardashir'. The verb is simple past third-person, narrating speech.
segments.s526-g62: که‌ای is an exclamatory/vocative construction (classical 'که ای ...') used to address someone: 'O ...'; شاه is the vocative 'king'; نیک‌اختر is an epithet literally 'good-starred' (auspicious) used as a form of address; و links the epithets; دلپذیر is an adjective 'pleasant/heart-pleasing'. Together it forms the priest's address: 'O king, auspicious and heart-pleasing.'
segments.s526-g63: سر شهریاری = 'the head/top of kingship' (a poetic way to say 'the throne/sovereignty'); همی + present stem + کنی is a classical Persian construction expressing an ongoing/habitual or near-future action addressed to 'you' (second person): همی نو کنی = 'you make new/you will renew'. The verb کنی is the present/subjunctive form used narratively.
segments.s526-g64: بر پارس = 'over/against Pars' (Pars = the province of Persia); باید = 'it is necessary / you must'; که introduces the clause of obligation; بی‌خو is an adjective 'sleepless' and کنی is again the second-person present/subjunctive 'you make/do'. The literal instruction is 'over Pars you must make (it) sleepless' — i.e., you must be sleepless/relentless regarding Pars (an idiomatic command to be tireless in ruling/conquering).
segments.s526-g9: بدانست is a past tense verb 'he realized/perceived' introducing a subordinate clause. کو here functions as a relative/archaised marker ('that/who') linking to the following negation; نیست means 'there is not/there was none'. جز is the excluding particle 'except', and کی نژاد is a nominal phrase 'of royal/Kayanian birth/lineage' (کی modifies نژاد), so the whole segment says 'he realized that there was no one except of royal lineage.'
segments.s526-g10: ز ... و ز ... is a double prepositional phrase 'from/of ... and from/of ...' giving cause or source. فر here means the divine/royal glory (farr) and اورنگ 'throne'; او is the 3rd-person pronoun referring back to the person of the preceding phrase, and گشت شاد is a simple past construction 'became glad' (گشت = became, شاد = happy). Together: 'who, by his farr and by his throne, became glad.'
segments.s526-g11: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. به دریا is the directional phrase 'to/toward the sea' (به + noun). هم adds 'also' or 'even'. اندر شتاب is a prepositional/advantage phrase 'in haste' (اندر = in, شتاب = haste), so the segment reads 'he too came to the sea, hastening.'
segments.s526-g12: به هر سو is a directional phrase 'toward every side' (به + هر سو). برافگند is a past form meaning 'threw/let cast' (here 'launched' or 'sent off'); زورق is the small boat/skiff being cast, and به آب specifies 'into the water'. Word order places verb before object (typical poetic order): 'he cast the skiff into the water to every side.'
segments.s526-g13: ز here indicates cause/reason 'because of/from'. آگاهی means 'knowledge/awareness' and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying آگاهی; اردشیر is the proper name 'Ardashir'. The whole phrase therefore reads 'because of Ardashir's renowned knowledge.'
segments.s526-g14: سپاه انجمن شد is a verbal clause 'the army/host became assembled' (سپاه = host, انجمن شد = 'was gathered/assembled'). بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon/at that', and آبگیر is the place 'pond/reservoir'; together: 'the host assembled at that reservoir.'
segments.s526-g15: هرانکس که introduces a conditional/relative clause 'whoever/whosoever (that)'. بد before a name expresses hostility or ill-treatment 'bad to/hostile toward', so بد بابکی reads 'hostile to Babak' or 'did ill to Babak'. در صطخر is a locative phrase 'in Istakhr' (در = in, صطخر = Istakhr).
segments.s526-g16: به آگاهی شاه is a prepositional phrase 'with/subject to the king's knowledge' (here: 'before the king' or 'to the king's awareness'). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' and فخر is the noun 'pride/boast' — together کرد‌ند فخر = 'they boasted' or 'they made a boast'. So the clause says '... boasted of it before the king.'
segments.s526-g33: چو introduces a conditional/protasis ('if'); باشم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of بودن used here in the protasis ('if I am' / 'if I prove to be'). از تخم is a genitival idiom 'of the seed/lineage of' (تخم = stock/seed) and links to اسفندیار (the ancestor). The whole segment reads 'if I am of Esfandiyar's line.'
segments.s526-g34: به + noun phrase marks direction/target: به مرز اندرون = 'to/into the border/limit of the inner part'. اندرون is an inside/inner-quarters noun placed after مرز to specify which border, and اردوان شهریار is the name + title ('Ardavan the king') modifying that place.
segments.s526-g35: سزد is an impersonal predicate 'it befits / it is proper'. گرد here functions adverbially ('about/with regard to') and مر 'me' gives the perspective ('for me' / 'as to me'). این را is the object ('this'), and نخوانیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive negative 'we should not recite/read'. داد likely means 'judgment / the matter in question' and is the object or appositive to 'این'.
segments.s526-g36: وزین is an adjective 'of weight / weighty' qualifying داستان 'tale / account'. کس is an indefinite 'anyone / any person'; نگیریم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative 'we do not take' (here 'take to heart' / 'make a memory of'); یاد is 'remembrance / memory' and is the object of نگیریم. Together: 'and from this weighty tale let no one take/remain any remembrance.'
segments.s526-g37: چو introduces a conditional 'if'; باشید is 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive ('you are' / 'you stand'). با من = 'with me'. بدین here is formed from the prefix ب- + the demonstrative/particle (an archaic/formal form) meaning 'with this/with such', and یارمند is an adjectival/noun 'well-attended / having allies' — so the phrase means roughly 'if you are with me with such allies.'
segments.s526-g38: نمانم is a negated form of ماندن used here in an optative/impersonal sense ('shall not remain / shall not be left'); به کس = 'to any person' (i.e. 'for anyone'); نام و تخت بلند is a compound object 'name (fame) and high/lofty throne'. The clause asserts that no one will be left with reputation or a lofty throne.
segments.s526-g39: چه گویید is an interrogative formula 'what do you say / what will you say' with گویید as 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/indicative 'you say'. و joins the clauses; این را is the direct object 'this', and چه پاسخ دهید is 'what answer will you give' (پاسخ = 'answer', دهید = 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/imperative 'give'). The sequence is a pair of direct questions.
segments.s526-g40: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that / so that'). پاسخ is 'answer' as subject/object; به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'with/by voice' (به used here in an adverbial/mediating sense), فرخ is an adjective 'happy/fortunate' qualifying آواز (hence 'in a joyous tone'), and نهید is the 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative of نهادن 'to place / to set / to utter' — 'you set forth / you give (the reply)'.
segments.s526-g49: This clause states possession/placement: "تن و جان ما" (our body and soul) is the subject, followed by the adverb "سربسر" meaning 'entirely/wholly'. "پیش" here means 'in the presence of / before (you)'. The verb-form "تست" is an archaic/poetic second-person copula ('are / belong to you') placed at the end — the clause order puts the subject and adverbial before the copula for emphasis.
segments.s526-g50: "غم و شادمانی" (sorrow and joy) is a coordinated noun phrase; "به کم بیش" is an adverbial quantitative phrase meaning 'to a lesser or greater degree' (literally 'by less, more'). The final "تست" again functions as the copula/predicate ('are yours' / 'are so'), so the clause says sorrow and joy are (subject to) you in small or large measure.
segments.s526-g51: The preposition "به" introduces the means/ground ('by/with two virtues'). "گوهر" is used metaphorically as 'virtue/essence' here. "از هرکسی برتری" is a comparative phrase: "برتری" (superiority) 'over/from everyone' ("از هرکسی"), so the whole segment says 'by two virtues you have superiority over everyone'.
segments.s526-g52: "سزد" is an impersonal/predictive verb meaning 'it befits' or 'is fitting'. The prepositional phrase "بر تو" ('upon you' = 'for you') marks the person to whom something befits, and "شاهی و کنداوری" are the things that befit him (subjects placed after the verb, a common inversion in Persian poetry). Grammatically the clause means 'kingship and Kandaavari befit you.'
segments.s526-g53: "به فرمان تو" is an instrumental/agent phrase 'at your command'. "کوه هامون کنیم" uses a verb-final clause: "کوه" (mountain) plus "هامون" (plain/hamun) indicate a transformation 'make mountains (into) hamun', and "کنیم" (1pl subjunctive/optative of کردن) expresses the speaker's willing action 'we will/may make'.
segments.s526-g54: "به تیغ" is an instrumental phrase 'with the sword'; "آب دریا" is a genitive construction 'the water of the sea'. "همه خون کنیم" places the quantifier "همه" before the noun "خون" and finishes with the verb "کنیم" — 'we will make all (of it) blood'. Again "کنیم" is 1pl expressing determined action.
segments.s526-g55: "چو" introduces a temporal clause 'when'. "پاسخ بدان گونه" is the object phrase 'an answer of that kind / such an answer' — "بدان" is an archaic fused form (here glossed as prefix+element) and "گونه" means 'manner/kind'. The verb "دید" (saw) precedes the subject "اردشیر" here (inversion common in poetic Persian): 'when Ardashir saw such a reply'.
segments.s526-g56: "سرش برتر آمد" is an idiomatic expression: literally 'his head came up' meaning 'his pride/esteem rose' or 'he became elated/superior'. "ز" here marks the standard of comparison ('than/from'), so "ز ناهید و تیر" compares him to Nahid and Tir — the clause therefore says his pride rose above Nahid and Tir. The verb "آمد" completes the idiom.

sentences.s526-s37: When the sun's summit rose above the mountain's ridge, it came toward Istakhr from before the water.
sentences.s526-s38: News reached Bahman Ardavan; his heart became full of pain and gloom.
sentences.s526-s39: He did not delay at all on the royal throne; he brought soldiers with the instruments of war.
sentences.s526-s21: Whoever was in that assembly — men of the sword and men of counsel.
sentences.s526-s22: When the call was heard they rose to their feet; everyone disclosed the secrets of his heart plainly.
sentences.s526-s23: — that whoever we are, descendants of Babak, we became joyful at the sight of your face.
sentences.s526-s24: And as for the rest of us, we are Sasanian; let us gird a belt of vengeance about our middles.
sentences.s526-s9: Others — whoever was born of Dara's seed — became renowned in every land.
sentences.s526-s10: When word came of King Ardashir, the old man's heart grew young with joy.
sentences.s526-s11: People kept coming from sea and mountain; young men, band after band, drew near.
sentences.s526-s12: From every city a sage-counselor came to the world-seeker; an assembly was formed before him.
sentences.s526-s13: The young Ardashir opened his tongue and said, “O famed, clear‑souled ones.”
sentences.s526-s14: There is no one among these famed members of the assembly — neither sage nor counselor of the people.
sentences.s526-s15: Who has not heard what Alexander the suspicious did of baseness in the world?
sentences.s526-s16: He slew our ancestors one by one and by cruelty brought the world into his fist.
sentences.s526-s1: From that side Ardashir reached the sea; to God he said thus, 'O helper...'
sentences.s526-s2: You have made me safe from the evildoer, so that the goodness of his person may never appear.
sentences.s526-s3: He rested, and summoned the helmsman forward; having left much of the work behind, he drove on.
sentences.s526-s4: The wise, old helmsman looked up and turned his face toward Ardashir.
sentences.s526-s33: After that, make war on Ardavan, for Akhtar is young and Khosrow is young.
sentences.s526-s34: For he surpasses the kings of the tribes in treasure, and from him were seen harm and suffering.
sentences.s526-s35: If/when you remove his place from its position, no one afterward will have footing with you from this saddle.
sentences.s526-s36: When proud-necked Ardashir heard those proper and pleasing words, ...
sentences.s526-s29: They lavished praise on those chiefs; in the heart, hatred spread from that thought.
sentences.s526-s30: Near the sea he founded a town, and the town became a marvel.
sentences.s526-s31: A certain priest said to Ardashir, 'O king, fortunate of star and heart-pleasing.'
sentences.s526-s32: You will renew the head of sovereignty; over Pars you must make (it) sleepless.
sentences.s526-s5: He realized that there was none except one of royal lineage who, from his farr and his throne, became glad.
sentences.s526-s6: He also came to the sea in haste; he launched the skiff into the water toward every side.
sentences.s526-s7: Because of Ardashir's renowned knowledge, the host assembled at that reservoir.
sentences.s526-s8: Whoever ill-treated Babak in Istakhr boasted of it before the king.
sentences.s526-s17: If I am of Esfandiyar's seed, I will drive the frontier into the inner quarters of King Ardavan.
sentences.s526-s18: It is fitting that, for me, we do not recite this judgment; and from this weighty tale let no one take remembrance.
sentences.s526-s19: If you stand with me, with such allies, no one shall be left with name or lofty throne.
sentences.s526-s20: What will you say, and what answer will you give, that you should deliver the reply in a joyful voice?
sentences.s526-s25: Our body and soul are wholly before you; sorrow and joy are in your hands, in lesser or greater measure.
sentences.s526-s26: By two virtues you excel everyone; kingship and Kandaavari are fitting for you.
sentences.s526-s27: At your command we will turn mountains into hamun (plains); with the sword we will make the sea's water all run with blood.
sentences.s526-s28: When Ardashir saw such an answer, his pride rose above Nahid and Tir.
